industryContext: Internet speeds are increasing globally. Digital streaming technology is emerging as a viable alternative to physical media. DVD rental by mail is currently the primary revenue source, but distribution costs are rising.

rounds.title: The Digital Pivot
rounds.description: The board is divided. DVD rentals are profitable today, but Blockbuster is struggling and the future looks digital. How do we approach the 'Watch Now' feature?

options.text: Double down on DVD distribution efficiency. It's our core competency.
options.lesson: Focusing on legacy cash cows can lead to the 'Innovator's Dilemma'.

teachMe.definition: Successful companies fail because they continue to do what made them successful, ignoring disruptive technologies.
teachMe.example: Kodak invented the digital camera but buried it to protect their highly profitable film business, eventually leading to bankruptcy.
teachMe.application: By doubling down on DVDs, you protected short-term margins but left the company vulnerable to digital disruption.

options.text: Invest 80% of R&D into a proprietary streaming platform immediately.
options.lesson: Aggressive digital transformation often requires short-term margin sacrifice for long-term scale.

teachMe.definition: The process of deciding how to distribute a company's financial resources to maximize shareholder value.
teachMe.example: Amazon consistently reinvested its cash flow into new infrastructure (AWS, logistics) rather than paying dividends, creating massive long-term value.
teachMe.application: You sacrificed short-term profitability to fund a massive pivot, betting that future scale will offset current losses.

options.text: Launch streaming as a free add-on for DVD subscribers to test demand.
options.lesson: Hybrid models allow for low-risk market testing before full-scale pivots.

teachMe.definition: The value of having the right, but not the obligation, to take a specific action in the future.
teachMe.example: Microsoft often builds 'me-too' products to test markets before committing massive resources to dominate them.
teachMe.application: You created a low-cost option to test streaming demand without cannibalizing your core DVD business.

rounds.title: Content Strategy
rounds.description: Competitors like Amazon and Hulu are entering the fray. Licensing costs from major studios are skyrocketing. How do we secure our library?

options.text: Pay the premium to keep Disney and Sony content. We need the hits.
options.lesson: Reliance on third-party IP creates high variable costs and platform fragility.

options.text: Start 'Netflix Originals'. Invest $100M in a high-end political drama (House of Cards).
options.lesson: Vertical integration into content production creates unique value and brand loyalty.

historicalReality: Netflix successfully transitioned from a DVD-by-mail service to the world's leading streaming platform. By investing in original content early and aggressively moving away from physical media, they disrupted the entire entertainment industry.

lessons.title: Disruptive Innovation
lessons.text: Incumbents often fail because they protect their current profit margins instead of embracing the technology that will replace them.

lessons.title: Content is King
lessons.text: In a platform war, owning the IP is the ultimate competitive advantage.

industryContext: The iPod is a massive success, but mobile phones are starting to include basic MP3 players. Motorola and Nokia dominate the handset market.

rounds.title: Project Purple
rounds.description: Steve Jobs is concerned that phones will 'eat' the iPod. We have two paths: a phone with a click-wheel (iPod+Phone) or a radical multi-touch screen.

options.text: Use the proven iPod click-wheel interface. It's what users know.
options.lesson: Iterative design is safer but rarely creates a category-defining product.

options.text: Invest in unproven multi-touch technology. It's risky but revolutionary.
options.lesson: Breakthrough innovation requires taking massive technical and user-experience risks.

historicalReality: Apple chose the multi-touch path (Project Purple), resulting in the iPhone in 2007. It redefined the smartphone and turned Apple into the most valuable company in the world.

lessons.title: Cannibalize Yourself
lessons.text: If you don't cannibalize your own successful products, someone else will.

lessons.title: User Experience as Moat
lessons.text: A superior interface can disrupt established hardware giants.

industryContext: Blockbuster is the king of video rentals with 9,000 stores. A small startup called Netflix has offered to sell itself to us for $50 million.

rounds.title: The Netflix Offer
rounds.description: Reed Hastings (Netflix CEO) is in our office. They want $50M. They do DVD-by-mail with no late fees. Our CFO thinks it's a joke.

options.text: Buy them. $50M is a rounding error for us. Secure the future.
options.lesson: Acquiring potential disruptors early is often the cheapest form of insurance.

options.text: Reject them. Our store network is our strength. Late fees are high-margin.
options.lesson: Over-reliance on high-margin but consumer-unfriendly practices (like late fees) creates vulnerability.

historicalReality: Blockbuster famously laughed Netflix out of the room. By 2010, Blockbuster was bankrupt, while Netflix was worth billions.

lessons.title: The Sunk Cost Fallacy
lessons.text: Blockbuster couldn't imagine a world without their expensive store network.

lessons.title: Customer Friction
lessons.text: If your business model relies on customer annoyance (late fees), you are ripe for disruption.

industryContext: The Model S and X are luxury hits, but to survive, Tesla needs a mass-market car: The Model 3. We are entering 'Production Hell'.

rounds.title: Automation vs. Human
rounds.description: Elon Musk wants to automate everything with 'The Alien Dreadnought' factory. But the robots are failing. Production is stalled.

options.text: Rip out the failing robots. Hire more humans to get the line moving.
options.lesson: Excessive automation can be a 'technical debt' that slows down physical manufacturing.

options.text: Keep the robots. Spend 24/7 debugging the code. We must automate to scale.
options.lesson: Scaling too many variables at once (new car + new factory + new automation) is a recipe for crisis.

historicalReality: Tesla eventually simplified the line, Musk slept on the factory floor, and they hit 5,000/week in 2018, narrowly avoiding bankruptcy.

lessons.title: Scaling is Hard
lessons.text: Prototypes are easy, production is hard.

lessons.title: Cash Runway
lessons.text: In high-growth manufacturing, time is your most expensive resource.

industryContext: Amazon is an e-commerce giant, but our internal infrastructure is a mess. We've built a set of internal tools to handle our own scale.

rounds.title: Selling the Infrastructure
rounds.description: Jeff Bezos suggests we sell our internal computing power to other companies as a service. It's a completely different business from retail.

options.text: Launch Amazon Web Services. Turn our cost center into a profit center.
options.lesson: Externalizing internal efficiencies can create entirely new, high-margin industries.

options.text: Keep the tech internal. We are a retailer, not a utility company.
options.lesson: Staying in your lane prevents distraction but misses massive 'adjacent' opportunities.

historicalReality: AWS was launched in 2006 and now provides the majority of Amazon's operating profit, powering much of the modern internet.

lessons.title: Infrastructure as a Service
lessons.text: The most valuable businesses often solve their own hardest problems and then sell the solution.

lessons.title: Margin Expansion
lessons.text: Low-margin businesses (retail) can use their scale to build high-margin businesses (cloud).

industryContext: Windows and Office sales are slowing. Mobile was a disaster. Satya Nadella has just taken over as CEO.

rounds.title: Cloud First, Mobile First
rounds.description: Do we keep protecting Windows, or do we put Office on the iPhone and focus entirely on the Azure cloud?

options.text: Windows is our moat. Keep Office exclusive to Windows devices.
options.lesson: Defending a declining monopoly often accelerates the decline.

options.text: Azure is the future. Make Office available everywhere (iOS/Android).
options.lesson: Embracing open ecosystems can revitalize legacy software giants.

historicalReality: Satya Nadella pivoted Microsoft to 'Cloud First', leading to a massive resurgence in valuation and Azure becoming a close second to AWS.

lessons.title: Cultural Transformation
lessons.text: Changing a company's strategy often requires changing its internal identity (from 'Windows' to 'Cloud').

lessons.title: Ecosystem Agnosticism
lessons.text: Software is more valuable when it runs on every device, not just your own.

industryContext: Cable TV (ESPN) is losing subscribers to 'cord-cutting'. Netflix is spending billions on content. We license our movies to them.

rounds.title: The Streaming War
rounds.description: Do we keep taking Netflix's money for our movies, or do we pull our content and launch our own service?

options.text: Pull content from Netflix. Launch Disney+ and go Direct-to-Consumer.
options.lesson: Direct-to-consumer relationships are more valuable than licensing fees in the long run.

options.text: Keep licensing. It's pure profit with no technical risk.
options.lesson: Short-term profit can blind a company to the loss of its distribution power.

historicalReality: Disney launched Disney+ in 2019, gaining 100M subscribers in record time, though the transition from cable profits remains a challenge.

lessons.title: DTC Transition
lessons.text: Owning the customer data and relationship is essential in the digital age.

lessons.title: Brand Power
lessons.text: Strong brands (Marvel, Star Wars) can overcome late entry into a market.

industryContext: Nokia is the #1 phone maker in the world. The iPhone has just been announced. Our Symbian OS is old but stable.

rounds.title: The Software Crisis
rounds.description: The iPhone is a computer in a pocket. Our OS is built for phones. Do we rebuild Symbian, or switch to a new platform?

options.text: Stick with Symbian. We have the scale and the manufacturing power.
options.lesson: Hardware dominance cannot save a company from a software paradigm shift.

options.text: Adopt the new 'Android' OS from Google. It's the best way to compete with Apple.
options.lesson: In a platform war, joining a winning ecosystem is better than dying alone on your own.

historicalReality: Nokia stuck with Symbian too long, then switched to Windows Phone, and eventually sold its mobile business to Microsoft after losing almost all market share.

lessons.title: Software is Eating the World
lessons.text: Nokia thought they were a hardware company; they were actually in the software business.

lessons.title: Speed of Collapse
lessons.text: Market leaders can go from 40% share to zero in less than 5 years during a paradigm shift.
